patterns.name: All and none
patterns.en: There is no food on the table.

patterns.name: Counting
patterns.phi: wi himo piru mua silawo nai.
patterns.gloss: two HUM.CLF trader LOC village be.
patterns.en: Two traders are in the village.

patterns.name: Politeness first
patterns.phi: pi wa mia po mua luphore wishe.
patterns.gloss: POL Q 1SG POT LOC river swim.
patterns.en: Please — may I swim in the river?

patterns.name: Place
patterns.en: The cat sleeps in a bed.

patterns.name: Recipient
patterns.en: The friend gives the child a flower.

patterns.name: Aspect
patterns.en: The parent has repaired the wall.

patterns.name: May and must
patterns.shape: po / na [verb], po ma / na ma
patterns.phi: lopia mua luphore sonu na ma wishe.
patterns.gloss: child LOC river alone NEC NEG swim.
patterns.en: The child must not swim in the river alone.

patterns.name: And, or
patterns.phi: wa siora sulopa sola milura nuola.
patterns.gloss: Q siora soup DISJ milk eat.
patterns.en: Will siora have soup or milk?

patterns.name: The embedded statement
patterns.phi: siora mena ne sulae so shua meno shane.
patterns.gloss: siora DECL.COMP NAME sulae FUT come DECL.COMP.CLOSE tell.
patterns.en: siora tells that sulae will come.

patterns.name: The embedded question
patterns.phi: lopia wela pheralu so lepa welo ma sano.
patterns.gloss: child INT.COMP rain FUT fall INT.COMP.CLOSE NEG know.
patterns.en: The child does not know whether it will rain.

patterns.name: The quotation
patterns.phi: mia shola muila theula howela sholo ro remo.
patterns.gloss: 1SG QUOT.COMP earth UNIV receive QUOT.COMP.CLOSE HAB think.
patterns.en: I still think over her words: the earth receives all.

patterns.name: The describing clause
patterns.en: The person who habitually makes soup.

patterns.name: If
patterns.phi: lu pheralu lepa. lo mia mua womu therilu.
patterns.gloss: COND rain fall. PL 1SG LOC home rest.
patterns.en: If rain falls, we rest at home.

patterns.name: The unreal if
patterns.phi: lu he mia pelori nai. mia wapi.
patterns.en: If I were a bird, I would fly.

patterns.name: The optative
patterns.en: May the community be at peace.

idioms.note: Phi has no empty it: the rain itself does the falling.
Phi has no empty it: the rain itself does the falling.
Phi has no empty it: the rain itself does the falling, and si holds it mid-fall.
Phi does not say the sun shines. It says the sun gives light — the primer calls this the language showing you its heart.
Phi does not say the sun shines. It says the sun gives light.

refusals.teach: Phi has no word for any weapon, and will never coin one. This is not a gap; it is the language's oldest refusal. When the Ring Verse was brought to Phi, the language could not comply — what came out instead was a small poem about a hearth. See the Ring Verse, refused, on the texts shelf.

Phi has no vocabulary of war. The language was built so that peace is easier to say than conflict — a speaker who wants to describe strife must describe the harm itself, plainly, and the grammar will not help them glorify it.
Phi has no direct word for killing. Harm can be described — things are harmful (peloma), broken, or unwell — but the language declines to make destruction easy to command.

Phi has no word for enemy. The nearest true statements name the relationship precisely: one who does not understand, one who acts harmfully. The Velveteen text says it best: only the people who do not understand see you as unbeautiful.

Phi refuses the vocabulary of domination — no lord, no master, no throne, no to-rule. This was settled by ruling: Schleicher's fable withholds the word master, describing only the one who takes. If your sentence is about guidance, Phi offers kulo (guide); if about care, theluo (steward), thema (guardian) — power as service, never as rank.
Phi refuses bind-as-captivity along with the rest of the domination vocabulary. What it can say is the harm itself, and the freeing.

Phi has no generic bad. Things are harmful (peloma), broken, or unwell — and the speaker must say which. The refusal is a discipline: name the actual problem, and the sentence becomes something that can be answered.
Hunt is refused by design. Phi's food vocabulary is the garden's and the market's; nunoa, the forager, gathers what the land gives.

Phi has no gendered person-words at all, by settled ruling: distinguishing genders anywhere in the language would exclude people outside the binary. miona (person), lopia (child), phao (parent), tewema (partner) carry the load equally for everyone. Try your sentence again with the person named by role or relationship.
